result.question:
FOCUSED follow-up run. What does peer-reviewed research establish about the EMOTIONAL / PSYCHOLOGICAL mechanisms of branding — consumer–brand emotional attachment, brand love, self–brand connection, brand experience, and processing fluency — well enough to codify the emotional layer of an evidence-based "branding" design skill? The brand-COLOUR science and brand-PERSONALITY (Aaker) strands are ALREADY confirmed in a prior run — do NOT re-derive them. Concentrate the entire effort on verifying the specific claims from these five already-identified primary sources:

1. Thomson, MacInnis & Park (2005), "The Ties That Bind: Measuring the Strength of Consumers' Emotional Attachments to Brands," Journal of Consumer Psychology 15(1):77-91 — the emotional-attachment construct (affection, passion, connection), its measurement, antecedents, and downstream effects (commitment, willingness to pay a price premium, willingness to sacrifice).
2. Park, MacInnis, Priester, Eisingerich & Iacobucci (2010), "Brand Attachment and Brand Attitude Strength: Conceptual and Empirical Differentiation of Two Critical Brand Equity Drivers," Journal of Marketing 74(6):1-17 — the attachment-aversion (AA) model; brand attachment vs brand-attitude strength as DISTINCT predictors of actual purchase behaviour, need-share, and brand purchase share.
3. Batra, Ahuvia & Bagozzi (2012), "Brand Love," Journal of Marketing 76(2):1-16 — the brand-love higher-order construct and its dimensions, and measured effects on loyalty, word of mouth, and resistance to negative information.
4. Brakus, Schmitt & Zarantonello (2009), "Brand Experience: What Is It? How Is It Measured? Does It Affect Loyalty?," Journal of Marketing 73(3):52-68 — the four-dimension brand-experience construct (sensory, affective, intellectual, behavioural), its scale, and its effect on satisfaction and loyalty (direct and via brand personality).
5. Reber, Schwarz & Winkielman (2004), "Processing Fluency and Aesthetic Pleasure: Is Beauty in the Perceiver's Processing Experience?," Personality and Social Psychology Review 8(4):364-382 — processing fluency as the cross-cutting mechanism driving liking / aesthetic pleasure, and whether the driver is fluency itself or the (mis)attribution of fluency to the stimulus.

CONSTRAINT: academic / peer-reviewed only (Journal of Marketing, JMR, JCR, Journal of Consumer Psychology, JAMS, Psychology & Marketing, Journal of Brand Management, PSPR). Name the study/author behind every claim. For each construct extract: (a) a crisp definition, (b) measured ANTECEDENTS, (c) measured DOWNSTREAM EFFECTS with the actual dependent variables (loyalty, willingness to pay, willingness to forgive / resist negative information, word of mouth, actual purchase / market share), and (d) how it differs from adjacent constructs (attachment vs attitude strength vs brand love vs satisfaction). Separate [STRONG] proven findings from [CONVENTION]; flag any [MYTH].

SYNTHESIS: a cited report that will MERGE with an already-confirmed colour-science + logo-design + brand-personality reference to complete an evidence-based "branding" skill. The skill stays subordinate to the accessibility / legibility / colour-contrast / chart-honesty floors of the sibling design-craft skills.
result.summary: Peer-reviewed consumer-psychology research robustly establishes THREE of the five requested emotional-branding mechanisms — each with a validated scale and actual-behavior outcomes — but the verified-claim set covers NONE of the other two. Confirmed: (1) emotional brand attachment (Thomson, MacInnis & Park 2005 — a 10-item affection/passion/connection scale that, in a model including rivals, was the ONLY significant predictor of willingness to pay a price premium beyond attitude, satisfaction, and involvement); (2) brand attachment as a self-bond (Park et al. 2010, with the 2013 AA-model sibling — brand-self connection/distance + prominence, a STRONGER predictor of actual purchase, brand purchase share, and need share than brand-attitude strength); and (3) brand love (Batra, Ahuvia & Bagozzi 2012 — a seven-element higher-order construct predicting loyalty, word of mouth, and resistance to negative information). These constructs are empirically DISTINCT from one another and from satisfaction/attitude: attachment is not a relabeling of attitude, attitude strength is the weaker behavioral predictor, and brand love is the broadest construct (it literally absorbs attitude valence AND attitude strength AND separation distress as sub-elements). The codifiable downstream signature: all three drive loyalty and advocacy, but only the ATTACHMENT constructs are demonstrated to drive price-premium willingness-to-pay and actual market-share behavior — and brand love specifically does NOT include willingness-to-pay (excluded for poor reliability), a misattribution the skill must avoid. CRITICAL GAP: brand experience (Brakus, Schmitt & Zarantonello 2009) and processing fluency (Reber, Schwarz & Winkielman 2004 — the cross-cutting liking mechanism that would tie the emotional layer to the legibility/contrast floors) produced ZERO verified claims in this run and remain uncodified.

findings.claim: [STRONG] Emotional brand attachment (Thomson, MacInnis & Park 2005) is the emotion-laden, target-specific bond connecting a consumer to a specific brand. It is operationalized by the FIRST validated scale of attachment STRENGTH: a 10-item instrument with three interrelated first-order factors loading on a single second-order EA construct — Affection (affectionate, loved, friendly, peaceful), Passion (passionate, delighted, captivated), and Connection (connected, bonded, attached). No validated scale of emotional-attachment strength existed before this paper; Studies 1-2 develop it, Studies 3-5 validate it.
sources: Thomson, MacInnis & Park (2005), 'The Ties That Bind,' Journal of Consumer Psychology 15(1):77-91 — https://myscp.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1207/s15327663jcp1501_10
Jimenez & Voss (2014), Psychology & Marketing 31(5) / Springer 10.1007/978-3-319-11797-3_10 (corroborating definition)
findings.evidence: Item-to-factor mapping (4+3+3=10) confirmed verbatim across primary + multiple independent secondary indexes. 'A scale to measure the strength of consumers' emotional attachments to brands had yet to be devised. We develop such a scale in Studies 1 and 2' is verbatim abstract text. Jimenez & Voss independently adopt the same emotional-bond definition while critiquing only the scale's dimensionality. ~2,575 citations; still the standard EA scale. One non-peer-reviewed blog claiming '15 items' was self-contradicting and overridden.

findings.claim: [STRONG] Emotional attachment's measured DOWNSTREAM EFFECTS are brand loyalty, willingness to pay a price premium, commitment, and investment (Rusbult-model accumulated resources; loosely, willingness to incur costs for the brand). These effects are INCREMENTAL beyond brand attitudes, satisfaction, and involvement: in the full SEM (Model 8) EA was the ONLY significant predictor of willingness to pay a premium (b=0.26, p<0.01), and remained a significant predictor of loyalty in a conservative hierarchical regression (b=0.14, p<0.02) with the rival predictors entered first.
sources: Jimenez & Voss / Springer 10.1007/978-3-319-11797-3_10 (corroborating loyalty + WTP as outcomes)
findings.evidence: Verbatim Study 5 results extracted from the authors' PDF. The authors' humility caveat ('we do not wish to suggest emotional attachment is the only driver of loyalty') is scoped to LOYALTY, not WTP — so 'only significant predictor of WTP premium' stands. Predictive validity for commitment + investment is verbatim abstract. Non-load-bearing nuance: 'investment' (sunk resources) is not identical to 'willingness to sacrifice.'

findings.claim: [STRONG] Emotional attachment is empirically DISTINCT (discriminant validity, Study 5 exploratory factor analysis with oblique rotation) from attitude favorability/valence, satisfaction, and involvement — it is not a relabeling of attitude or satisfaction. Scope note: Study 5 tested attitude VALENCE; the separate distinction from attitude STRENGTH is Park et al. (2010)'s contribution (see Finding 6).
findings.evidence: The discriminant-validity sentence is reproduced near-verbatim across 5+ independent indexes (Wiley, ScienceDirect, Semantic Scholar, scite, scispace, ResearchGate). No contradicting source; the rival Voss & Jimenez scale critiques only DIMENSIONALITY, not the distinction from satisfaction/involvement/attitude, and itself establishes discriminant validity from attitude.

findings.claim: [STRONG — definition] Park, MacInnis, Priester, Eisingerich & Iacobucci (2010) define brand attachment as the STRENGTH of the bond connecting the brand with the SELF, exemplified by a rich, accessible memory network. It has two constituent factors: brand-self CONNECTION and brand PROMINENCE — prominence being the perceived ease and frequency with which brand-related thoughts and feelings are brought to mind (i.e., accessibility/salience).
sources: Park, MacInnis, Priester, Eisingerich & Iacobucci (2010), 'Brand Attachment and Brand Attitude Strength,' Journal of Marketing 74(6):1-17 — https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.1509/jmkg.74.6.1
findings.evidence: Verbatim from the primary PDF (p.2), including the prominence = ease + frequency operationalization. Reproduced identically in a 2022 Journal of Brand Management review and AMS Review. No credible contradiction. (The 2013 AA paper later swaps 'connection' for 'distance' to span aversion — see Finding 7.)

findings.claim: [STRONG — actual behavior] Brand attachment is a STRONGER predictor of ACTUAL consumer behavior than brand attitude strength, with incremental predictive validity over attitude strength on four real-world dependent variables: (1) intentions to perform difficult/resource-using behaviors (time, money, reputation — the 'willingness to sacrifice' analogue), (2) actual purchase behaviors, (3) brand purchase share (share among directly competing brands), and (4) need share (reliance on the brand for relevant needs). Effects hold after controlling for consumer inertia (past behavior); the measure shows convergent AND discriminant validity vs attitude strength.
sources: Park et al. (2010), Journal of Marketing 74(6):1-17 — https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.1509/jmkg.74.6.1
ScienceDaily 2010-11-04 (reporting the published JM study, corroboration)
findings.evidence: All four DVs named verbatim in the abstract; this is the paper's central thesis and literal title ('Conceptual and Empirical Differentiation of Two Critical Brand Equity Drivers'). Top-tier JM, ~4,000 citations. 'Stronger predictor' is correctly scoped to consequential/effortful behaviors. Follow-on 'dark side' papers extend rather than refute. Full-text PDF was 403-blocked; verification rests on the verbatim abstract + secondary corroboration.

findings.claim: [STRONG — conceptual boundary] Brand attachment and brand attitude strength are conceptually distinct: attachment varies by the STRENGTH of the self-brand bond (not attitude valence), is largely TIME-dependent, and reflects a more advanced relationship stage; strong attitudes can form quickly via elaboration and need NOT be time-dependent. They are two distinct critical brand-equity drivers.
sources: Park et al. (2010), Journal of Marketing 74(6):1-17 — https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.1509/jmkg.74.6.1
Park, MacInnis & Priester, 'Brand Attachment: Constructs, Consequences, and Causes,' Foundations & Trends in Marketing (companion monograph, corroboration)
findings.evidence: 'What varies is not the valence of the attachment but rather the strength of the bond... whereas attachment is largely time dependent, brand attitude strength need not be' is verbatim from the published PDF. Companion monograph corroborates the elaboration-driven attitude-strength and over-time attachment paraphrases. The known statistical-separability debate (moderate-high correlation) concerns empirical separability, NOT this conceptual boundary.

findings.claim: [STRONG — definition + actual behavior] The formal Attachment-Aversion (AA) model (Park, Eisingerich & Park 2013, JCP — a SIBLING of, not identical to, the 2010 JM paper) operationalizes customer-brand relationships via two dimensions: perceived brand-self DISTANCE (relationship valence, spanning positive attachment to negative aversion) and brand PROMINENCE (relationship salience/accessibility of brand memories). It specifies three determinants (enticing/gratifying, enabling, and enriching the self) and predicts both behavioral INTENTIONS and ACTUAL brand behaviors (e.g., repeat purchase) via a mediating process.
sources: Park, Eisingerich & Park (2013), 'Attachment-Aversion (AA) Model of Customer-Brand Relationships,' Journal of Consumer Psychology 23(2):229-248 — https://www.researchgate.net/publication/257474486_Attachment-aversion_AA_model_of_customer-brand_relationships
findings.evidence: Two-dimension mapping (distance=valence, prominence=salience) is a verbatim abstract match with no axis swap; three determinants and 'behavioral intentions and actual brand behaviors' are verbatim. Important non-conflation: 2013 uses brand-self DISTANCE (spans aversion); 2010 used brand-self CONNECTION (positive only). Outcome chain is partly MEDIATED (AA → intentions → behaviors), so 'predict' = confirmed nomological effect, not two independent direct paths.

findings.claim: The AA Relationships measure is presented as empirically DISTINCT from — and OUTPERFORMING — three adjacent constructs tested side-by-side: brand attachment, emotional valence, and brand attitude strength, across a set of dependent variables. This corroborates the attachment-vs-attitude-strength differentiation, though as a secondary contribution of the 2013 paper rather than its headline.
findings.evidence: The three comparison constructs are named verbatim in the abstract, and the paper's purpose statement uses 'predict better than.' But the paywalled results tables (effect-size magnitudes per DV) were inaccessible, so 'outperforms' rests on abstract/purpose wording, not the numbers. Published commentaries (Alba & Lutz; Fournier & Alvarez) dispute the broad aversion/relationship scope but AFFIRM the attachment-vs-attitude-strength distinction. The primary actual-purchase/market-share evidence for that distinction remains the 2010 JM paper (Finding 5).

findings.claim: [STRONG — definition] Batra, Ahuvia & Bagozzi (2012) establish brand love as a HIGHER-ORDER prototype construct (NOT a single emotion), composed of seven core elements: self-brand integration, passion-driven behaviors, positive emotional connection, long-term relationship, positive overall attitude valence, attitude certainty/confidence (strength), and anticipated separation distress.
sources: Batra, Ahuvia & Bagozzi (2012), 'Brand Love,' Journal of Marketing 76(2):1-16 — https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.1509/jm.09.0339
findings.evidence: All seven elements reproduce the abstract verbatim, in exact order, including the parenthetical '(strength)'. Grounded-theory/prototype methodology + first-order and higher-order SEM. ~1,500 citations; canonical. Critiques (Rossiter's measure critique; the four-continents J. Business Research replication) target generalizability/managerial utility, NOT the seven-element model the paper established. Note the construct internally absorbs attitude valence AND attitude strength AND separation distress — central to differentiation (Finding 11).

findings.claim: [STRONG] The brand-love prototype places perceived HIGH QUALITY (quality beliefs) as the ANTECEDENT, and three downstream effects modeled JOINTLY as one consequence construct ('loyalty-WOM-resistance,' LWOMR): brand loyalty, positive word of mouth, and resistance to negative information. Both first-order and higher-order brand-love models predict these BETTER than a single overall summary measure of love. Willingness to pay a price premium is NOT an outcome here — it was excluded for poor reliability — so attributing WTP to brand love (per this paper) is a misattribution; WTP belongs to Thomson 2005 / Park 2010.
sources: Batra, Ahuvia & Bagozzi (2012), Journal of Marketing 76(2):1-16 — https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.1509/jm.09.0339
findings.evidence: The final verifier decoded the PDF's custom +55-offset font and confirmed verbatim: quality antecedent, the named 'LWOMR' joint consequence construct, the 'predict better than an overall summary measure' clause, and WTP 'excluded because of poor reliability.' The loyalty/WOM/resistance outcomes are also independently corroborated 3-0 via claim 21. CONFIDENCE NUANCE: the precise 'exactly three / WTP-excluded / single-joint-construct' framing came from split (1-1) votes, but the deciding verification was high-evidence primary-PDF decode — treat as high-evidence with the split noted.
findings.vote: Merges claims 22,24 — both split 1-1, resolved by high-evidence primary-PDF verification

findings.claim: [SYNTHESIS — derived from the unanimous primary findings] The emotional-branding constructs form a differentiated hierarchy, not synonyms, which the skill can codify as a ladder: (1) satisfaction and brand attitude (valence) are the most cognitive/weakest — attachment is empirically distinct from both; (2) attitude STRENGTH is distinct from attachment and the weaker predictor of actual/effortful behavior; (3) emotional attachment (Thomson, affective bond) and brand attachment (Park, self-bond) are the affective constructs that UNIQUELY predict price-premium willingness-to-pay and actual purchase/market-share; (4) brand love (Batra) is the broadest higher-order construct, subsuming attachment-like emotional connection PLUS attitude valence/strength PLUS separation distress. Shared downstream signature across all: loyalty + word of mouth/advocacy. Distinguishing signature: only the attachment constructs are demonstrated to drive price-premium WTP and actual market-share behavior.
sources: Park et al. (2010), Journal of Marketing 74(6):1-17
Batra, Ahuvia & Bagozzi (2012), Journal of Marketing 76(2):1-16
findings.evidence: Derived by integrating the discriminant-validity results (Thomson Study 5 vs satisfaction/involvement/attitude; Park 2010 convergent+discriminant vs attitude strength) with the differential dependent-variable sets across the four papers. Each component is independently 3-0 verified above; the integration adds no new empirical claim, only the relational map the skill needs for part (d) of the question.

result.caveats:
COVERAGE GAP IS THE HEADLINE: this run verified only THREE of the five requested constructs. Brand experience (Brakus, Schmitt & Zarantonello 2009 — the four-dimension sensory/affective/intellectual/behavioural construct, its scale, and its effect on satisfaction/loyalty direct and via brand personality) and processing fluency (Reber, Schwarz & Winkielman 2004 — whether liking is driven by fluency itself or by misattribution of fluency to the stimulus) produced ZERO surviving claims. Processing fluency is the single most important omission: it is the cross-cutting mechanism that would connect this emotional layer to the legibility/contrast/typography FLOORS of the sibling design-craft skills, and it is entirely absent here. The emotional layer cannot be considered complete on the basis of this run alone.

STRONG-vs-CONVENTION-vs-MYTH: every surviving claim is [STRONG] (primary-source, hypothesis-tested). There are no [CONVENTION] items and only one [MYTH] guardrail — do NOT attribute willingness-to-pay/price-premium to brand love per Batra 2012 (it was excluded for poor reliability). This uniformity reflects the verifier killing weak claims, not an absence of conventions/myths in the wider literature.

SOURCE-ACCESS LIMITS: several verifications relied on verbatim ABSTRACTS plus independent secondary indexes rather than full body text (SAGE/SSRN returned HTTP 403; two PDFs used custom subset-font encoding, one decoded by reversing a +55 offset). Effect-size magnitudes for the 2013 AA 'outperforms' element come from abstract/purpose statements, not the paywalled results tables — hence that finding is medium, not high.

DO-NOT-CONFLATE: the 2010 paper (brand-self CONNECTION + prominence, positive-only) and the 2013 AA paper (brand-self DISTANCE + prominence, spans aversion) use different second constructs. The research question's 'source #2' is the 2010 JM paper; the 2013 JCP AA paper is its sibling, included as corroboration.

CORRELATIONAL CEILING: findings are SEM/survey-based (with some actual-behavior DVs in Park 2010/2013). None show that a specific DESIGN move CAUSES attachment or love. The skill must separately bridge 'these constructs drive outcomes' to 'design choices that build them' — the verified set is thin on measured ANTECEDENTS (only Batra's 'quality beliefs').
openQuestions: Brand experience (Brakus, Schmitt & Zarantonello 2009, JM 73(3):52-68): what do the four verified dimensions (sensory, affective, intellectual, behavioural) and the scale establish about effects on satisfaction and loyalty — and is the claim that brand experience affects loyalty PARTLY VIA brand personality confirmed? No claims survived; this needs a dedicated follow-up run before the emotional layer ships.

Processing fluency (Reber, Schwarz & Winkielman 2004, PSPR 8(4):364-382): is aesthetic liking driven by fluency itself or by the MISATTRIBUTION of fluency to the stimulus, and which manipulations (symmetry, figure-ground contrast, prototypicality, priming/repetition) reliably raise it? This is the mechanism that would link the emotional layer to the skill's legibility/contrast floors — entirely unverified here.

Measured ANTECEDENTS / design levers: beyond Batra's 'quality beliefs,' what design-controllable inputs (visual identity, sensory cues, self-congruence/self-expression) are empirically shown to BUILD attachment or love, rather than merely correlate with their outcomes?

Integration with the already-confirmed strands: how do these emotional constructs interact with the confirmed brand-personality (Aaker) and brand-colour science — specifically, is brand personality the mediator between brand experience and loyalty (Brakus 2009's natural merge point with the personality strand), and does colour/visual fluency feed the attachment pathway?
